def feature_counts(
    self,
    gtf_file: str | Path | None = None,
    bed_file: str | Path | None = None,
    ranges: Any | None = None,
    feature_type: FeatureType | str = FeatureType.GENE,
    aggregation: str | None = None,
    assays: list[str] | None = None,
    integerize: bool = False,
) -> tuple[pd.DataFrame, pd.DataFrame]:
    """
    Generate feature count matrix compatible with DESeq2.

    Parameters
    ----------
    gtf_file : str or Path, optional
        Path to GTF file for feature extraction.
    bed_file : str or Path, optional
        Path to BED file with genomic ranges.
    ranges : DataFrame or PyRanges, optional
        Pre-parsed genomic ranges (DataFrame or PyRanges).
    feature_type : FeatureType or str, default FeatureType.GENE
        GTF feature type to extract (e.g., 'gene', 'transcript', 'exon').
    aggregation : str, optional
        How to aggregate sub-features (e.g., 'sum' to merge exons into genes).
    assays : list of str, optional
        Which assays to include in output (e.g., ['sum']).
    integerize : bool, default False
        If True, round counts to nearest integer for DESeq2.

    Returns
    -------
    counts : DataFrame
        Count matrix (features x samples).
    features : DataFrame
        Feature metadata (name, length, etc.).

    Examples
    --------
    >>> qn = QuantNado.open("store.zarr")
    >>> counts, features = qn.feature_counts("genes.gtf", feature_type="gene")
    >>> counts.shape
    (n_genes, n_samples)
    """
    return _feature_counts(
        self.store,
        gtf_file=gtf_file,
        bed_file=bed_file,
        ranges_df=ranges,
        feature_type=feature_type,
        aggregate_by=aggregation,
        assay=assays[0] if assays else None,
        integerize=integerize,
    )

docs.title: pca
docs.text:
pca(data: DataArray, n_components: int = 10, nan_handling_strategy: str = 'drop') -> tuple[Any, xr.DataArray]

Run PCA on reduced genomic signal data.

This method performs dimensionality reduction on feature-level signal (typically from .reduce()) for visualization and quality control.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default data DataArray

Input data with dimensions (feature, sample). Typically output from .reduce() method (e.g., reduced["mean"]).

required n_components int

Number of principal components to compute.

10 nan_handling_strategy str

How to handle NaN values. Options: - "drop": Remove features with any NaN - "set_to_zero": Replace NaN with 0 - "mean_value_imputation": Replace NaN with feature mean

"drop"

Returns:

Name Type Description pca_obj PCA

Fitted PCA object.

transformed DataArray

Transformed data with dimensions (sample, component). Coordinates include sample names and component indices.

Examples:

>>> qn = QuantNado.open("store.zarr")
>>> reduced = qn.reduce("promoters.bed", reduction="mean")
>>> pca, transformed = qn.pca(reduced["mean"], n_components=10)
>>> transformed.shape
(n_samples, 10)
Source code in quantnado/api.py 
def pca(
    self,
    data: xr.DataArray,
    n_components: int = 10,
    nan_handling_strategy: str = "drop",
) -> tuple[Any, xr.DataArray]:
    """
    Run PCA on reduced genomic signal data.

    This method performs dimensionality reduction on feature-level signal
    (typically from `.reduce()`) for visualization and quality control.

    Parameters
    ----------
    data : DataArray
        Input data with dimensions (feature, sample). Typically output
        from `.reduce()` method (e.g., `reduced["mean"]`).
    n_components : int, default 10
        Number of principal components to compute.
    nan_handling_strategy : str, default "drop"
        How to handle NaN values. Options:
        - "drop": Remove features with any NaN
        - "set_to_zero": Replace NaN with 0
        - "mean_value_imputation": Replace NaN with feature mean

    Returns
    -------
    pca_obj : sklearn.decomposition.PCA
        Fitted PCA object.
    transformed : DataArray
        Transformed data with dimensions (sample, component).
        Coordinates include sample names and component indices.

    Examples
    --------
    >>> qn = QuantNado.open("store.zarr")
    >>> reduced = qn.reduce("promoters.bed", reduction="mean")
    >>> pca, transformed = qn.pca(reduced["mean"], n_components=10)
    >>> transformed.shape
    (n_samples, 10)
    """
    return _run_pca(
        data,
        n_components=n_components,
        nan_handling_strategy=nan_handling_strategy,
    )

docs.title: Command-Line Interface
docs.text: QuantNado provides command-line tools for common workflows, accessible via the main quantnado command.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
All QuantNado operations can be performed from the terminal without writing Python code:

quantnado --help

docs.title: Available Commands
docs.text:
QuantNado provides the following commands:

Command Purpose Use Case create-dataset Convert BAM files to Zarr Initial data processing call-peaks Call peaks from bigWig files Peak identification

docs.title: General Options
docs.text:
All commands support these options:

  • --help, -h - Show command help and usage
  • --verbose, -v - Enable debug logging
  • --log-file - Path to save logs

docs.title: Quick Start: Create Dataset
docs.text:
quantnado create-dataset sample1.bam sample2.bam \
  --output dataset.zarr \
  --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes

docs.title: Quick Start: Call Peaks
docs.text:
quantnado call-peaks \
  --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ \
  --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes \
  --quantile 0.98

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
Get help for any command:

quantnado --help                    # Main help
quantnado create-dataset --help     # Create dataset help
quantnado call-peaks --help         # Peak calling help

docs.title: Current Limitations
docs.text:
• BAM file input requires local filesystem access
  • Zarr output can be written to local or remote storage
  • Large datasets may require significant disk space (see Storage Requirements)

docs.title: Call Peaks Command
docs.text: Call quantile-based peaks from bigWig files.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: quantnado call-peaks [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The call-peaks command identifies peaks in bigWig files using quantile-based thresholding. It's useful for determining enriched regions from ChIP-seq, ATAC-seq, and similar assays.

docs.title: BigWig Directory (--bigwig-dir)
docs.text:
Required. Directory containing bigWig (.bw) files:

quantnado call-peaks --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ --output-dir ./peaks/ --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes

All .bw files in the directory will be processed.

docs.title: Output Directory (--output-dir)
docs.text:
Required. Directory to save peak files in BED format:

quantnado call-peaks \
  --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ \
  --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes

Output BED files will be named: <bigwig_name>.peaks.bed

docs.title: Chromsizes (--chromsizes)
docs.text:
Required. Path to chromsizes file:

quantnado call-peaks \
  --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ \
  --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes /reference/hg38.chrom.sizes

docs.title: Quantile (--quantile)
docs.text:
Quantile threshold for peak calling (default: 0.98):

# Stricter threshold - fewer, higher-confidence peaks
quantnado call-peaks --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes --quantile 0.99

# More relaxed threshold - more peaks
quantnado call-peaks --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes --quantile 0.95

Higher quantile = fewer peaks (more stringent)

Quantile Interpretation 0.90 90th percentile - relaxed 0.95 95th percentile - standard 0.98 98th percentile - stringent 0.99 99th percentile - very stringent

docs.title: Tile Size (--tilesize)
docs.text:
Size of genomic tiles for peak calling (default: 128 bp):

quantnado call-peaks --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes --tilesize 50

Smaller tiles = more sensitive to local changes Larger tiles = more robust to noise

docs.title: Blacklist (--blacklist)
docs.text:
Path to BED file with regions to exclude:

quantnado call-peaks \
  --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ \
  --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes \
  --blacklist /reference/hg38-blacklist.bed

Blacklist format (tab-separated): 

chr1    1000    2000
chr2    5000    6000

docs.title: Merge (--merge / --no-merge)
docs.text:
Merge overlapping peaks (default: disabled with --no-merge):

# Enable merging
quantnado call-peaks \
  --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ \
  --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes \
  --merge

# Disable merging (default)
quantnado call-peaks \
  --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ \
  --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes \
  --no-merge

docs.title: Temporary Directory (--tmp-dir)
docs.text:
Temporary directory for intermediate files (default: tmp):

quantnado call-peaks \
  --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ \
  --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes \
  --tmp-dir /scratch/tmp

docs.title: Log File (--log-file)
docs.text:
Save logs (default: quantnado_peaks.log):

quantnado call-peaks \
  --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ \
  --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes \
  --log-file peak_calling.log

docs.title: Verbose (--verbose, -v)
docs.text:
Enable debug logging:

quantnado call-peaks \
  --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ \
  --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes \
  --verbose

docs.title: Basic Peak Calling
docs.text:
Call peaks from bigWig files:

quantnado call-peaks \
  --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ \
  --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes

docs.title: Stringent Thresholding
docs.text:
Call only high-confidence peaks:

quantnado call-peaks \
  --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ \
  --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes \
  --quantile 0.99 \
  --merge

docs.title: With Blacklist
docs.text:
Exclude problematic regions:

quantnado call-peaks \
  --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ \
  --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes \
  --blacklist /reference/hg38-blacklist.bed \
  --quantile 0.98

docs.title: Fine-Tuned Tile Size
docs.text:
Use smaller tiles for better resolution:

quantnado call-peaks \
  --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ \
  --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes \
  --tilesize 50 \
  --quantile 0.98

docs.title: Full Pipeline
docs.text:
Comprehensive peak calling with all options:

quantnado call-peaks \
  --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ \
  --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes /reference/hg38.chrom.sizes \
  --blacklist /reference/hg38-blacklist.bed \
  --quantile 0.98 \
  --tilesize 128 \
  --merge \
  --tmp-dir /scratch/tmp \
  --log-file peak_calling.log \
  --verbose

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
Output BED files contain called peaks:

chr1    1000    2000    peak_1    100    .
chr1    5000    6500    peak_2    150    .
chr2    10000   11000   peak_3    120    .

Columns: 1. Chromosome 2. Start position 3. End position 4. Peak name 5. Score (signal intensity) 6. Strand (always . for this implementation)

docs.title: Typical Run Times
docs.text:
Peak calling time depends on bigWig file size:

Genome Coverage Time Low coverage 1-5 minutes Medium coverage 5-15 minutes High coverage 15-30 minutes

docs.title: Resource Requirements
docs.text:
• Memory: 2-8 GB (depends on tile size and coverage)
  • Disk: ~2-3× bigWig file size (for temporary files)

docs.title: No bigWig files found
docs.text:
Problem: Directory is empty or wrong format

Solution: Check directory and file extensions: 

ls -l ./bigwigs/*.bw

docs.title: Missing chromsizes
docs.text:
Problem: FileNotFoundError: chromsizes file not found

Solution: Download or provide chromsizes: 

# Download from UCSC
wget http://hgdownload.cse.ucsc.edu/goldenpath/hg38/bigZips/hg38.chrom.sizes

docs.title: Out of memory
docs.text:
Problem: Memory error during peak calling

Solution: Use larger tile size (fewer tiles): 

quantnado call-peaks \
  --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ \
  --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes \
  --tilesize 256

docs.title: No peaks detected
docs.text:
Problem: Output peak files are empty

Likely causes: - Quantile threshold too stringent - BigWig files have low signal - Incorrect chromsizes

Solution: Lower quantile threshold: 

quantnado call-peaks \
  --bigwig-dir ./bigwigs/ \
  --output-dir ./peaks/ \
  --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes \
  --quantile 0.90

docs.title: Preparing BigWig Files
docs.text:
Convert BAM to bigWig if needed:

# Create bedGraph
bedtools genomecov -ibam sample.bam -bg -o sample.bedgraph

# Convert to bigWig
bedGraphToBigWig sample.bedgraph hg38.chrom.sizes sample.bw

docs.title: Python Equivalent
docs.text: There's currently no direct Python API for peak calling. Use the CLI command.

docs.title: Create Dataset Command
docs.text: Convert BAM files into a Zarr dataset for efficient analysis.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: quantnado create-dataset [OPTIONS] BAM_FILES...

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The create-dataset command processes one or more BAM files and creates a Zarr-backed dataset for efficient genomic signal analysis. This is the first step in most QuantNado workflows.

docs.title: BAM Files (Required)
docs.text:
Paths to BAM files to process (one or more):

quantnado create-dataset sample1.bam sample2.bam sample3.bam --output dataset.zarr

BAM files must be: - Coordinate-sorted - Indexed (.bai file in same directory) - Valid BAM format

docs.title: Output (--output, -o)
docs.text:
Required. Path where the Zarr dataset will be saved:

quantnado create-dataset *.bam --output dataset.zarr

Creates a directory dataset.zarr/ containing the processed data.

docs.title: Chromsizes (--chromsizes)
docs.text:
Path to chromsizes file or auto-detect from first BAM:

# Specify explicit chromsizes
quantnado create-dataset *.bam --output dataset.zarr --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes

# Auto-detect from BAM (default)
quantnado create-dataset *.bam --output dataset.zarr

Chromsizes file format (tab-separated): 

chr1    248956422
chr2    242193529
...

docs.title: Metadata (--metadata)
docs.text:
Path to metadata CSV file:

quantnado create-dataset *.bam --output dataset.zarr --metadata samples.csv

CSV format with sample_id column: 

sample_id,condition,replicate,quality
sample1,control,1,high
sample2,control,2,high
sample3,treatment,1,high

docs.title: Max Workers (--max-workers)
docs.text:
Number of parallel worker threads (default: 4):

# Use more workers for faster processing (uses more memory)
quantnado create-dataset *.bam --output dataset.zarr --max-workers 8

# Use single worker for memory efficiency
quantnado create-dataset *.bam --output dataset.zarr --max-workers 1

docs.title: Overwrite (--overwrite)
docs.text:
Overwrite existing dataset at same path (default: no overwrite):

quantnado create-dataset *.bam --output dataset.zarr --overwrite

docs.title: Log File (--log-file)
docs.text:
Path to save processing logs (default: quantnado_processing.log):

quantnado create-dataset *.bam --output dataset.zarr --log-file processing.log

docs.title: Verbose (--verbose, -v)
docs.text:
Enable debug logging:

quantnado create-dataset *.bam --output dataset.zarr --verbose

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
Create dataset from BAM files:

quantnado create-dataset sample1.bam sample2.bam sample3.bam \
  --output my_dataset.zarr

docs.title: With Chromsizes
docs.text:
Specify explicit chromsizes file:

quantnado create-dataset *.bam \
  --output dataset.zarr \
  --chromsizes /reference/hg38.chrom.sizes

docs.title: With Metadata
docs.text:
Include sample metadata:

quantnado create-dataset *.bam \
  --output dataset.zarr \
  --metadata samples.csv

docs.title: Parallel Processing
docs.text:
Use multiple workers for faster processing:

quantnado create-dataset *.bam \
  --output dataset.zarr \
  --max-workers 8 \
  --verbose

docs.title: Resume Processing
docs.text:
Resume interrupted dataset creation:

# Automatically resumes from where it left off
quantnado create-dataset *.bam --output dataset.zarr

docs.title: Typical Run Times
docs.text:
Creation time depends on sequencing depth:

Read Count Time (single sample) 5M reads 1-2 minutes 50M reads 5-10 minutes 100M+ reads 15-30 minutes

docs.title: Output Size
docs.text:
Zarr dataset size approximates BAM file size:

Read Depth Size 5M reads 500 MB 50M reads 5 GB 100M+ reads 10+ GB

docs.title: BAM file errors
docs.text:
Problem: FileNotFoundError: BAM file not found

Solution: Verify path and ensure .bai index exists: 

ls -l sample1.bam sample1.bam.bai
samtools index sample1.bam

docs.title: Chromsizes errors
docs.text:
Problem: ValueError: chromsizes_dict appears empty

Solution: Provide explicit chromsizes file: 

quantnado create-dataset *.bam --output dataset.zarr --chromsizes hg38.chrom.sizes

docs.title: Memory issues
docs.text:
Problem: Out of memory during processing

Solution: Use fewer workers: 

quantnado create-dataset *.bam --output dataset.zarr --max-workers 1

docs.title: Slow processing
docs.text:
Problem: Dataset creation is very slow

Solution: Use more workers (if memory available): 

quantnado create-dataset *.bam --output dataset.zarr --max-workers 8

docs.title: Python Equivalent
docs.text:
The CLI create-dataset command is equivalent to:

from quantnado import QuantNado
import pandas as pd

qn = QuantNado.from_bam_files(
    bam_files=["sample1.bam", "sample2.bam", "sample3.bam"],
    store_path="dataset.zarr",
    chromsizes="hg38.chrom.sizes",
    metadata="samples.csv",
    overwrite=True
)

See Dataset Creation Guide for Python API details.
